confidence_scale.CONFIRMED: Primary source documentary record cited — burial index, vital record, named newspaper, published genealogy, or encyclopedia entry
confidence_scale.PROBABLE: 2+ corroborating secondary sources, no single record states the full claim
confidence_scale.POSSIBLE: Plausible from single source or logical inference
confidence_scale.NOT_FOUND: Searched, not found in this pass

hunters_connection: Paternal line: Hunter ← Dale William Spence Jr. ← Dr. Dale William Spence Sr. ← Dovie (Byrd) Spence ← John A Byrd (1868 AL / 1928 TX) ← William Leander Byrd (1832 AL) ← [further uncertain generations back to colonial Virginia]

candidate_1.label: John Archie Asner Byrd (1868 Birmingham AL) + Martha Alice Bedford

john_a_byrd_confirmed_records.record: Burial: Sharp Cemetery, Sharp, Milam County, Texas
john_a_byrd_confirmed_records.note: PeopleLegacy lists birthplace as Texas; Ancestry records list birthplace as Birmingham, Alabama — small discrepancy in birth state, exact date consistent across sources

john_a_byrd_confirmed_records.parents_per_ancestry: Father: William Leander Byrd; Mother: Margarete Rhetta (Perideaux) Peradeau
john_a_byrd_confirmed_records.note: This was returned in two independent Ancestry searches in this research pass. This is NOT a primary source — it reflects data entered by Ancestry tree contributors — but it is a significant corroboration of the GEDCOM line.

candidate_1.martha_alice_bedford_status: UNVERIFIED — no independent record of Martha Alice Bedford as John A Byrd's wife found in this research pass. Ancestry records name his mother as Margarete Rhetta Perideaux (consistent with William Leander Byrd marriage). Martha Alice Bedford may be John A Byrd's wife (i.e., Dovie's mother), not his mother.
candidate_1.dovie_as_daughter_of_john_a_byrd: POSSIBLE — no primary source (census, birth record, death record) confirms Dovie as John A Byrd's daughter. Burial proximity of related Byrds at Sharp Cemetery is circumstantially consistent.

candidate_2.label: Lillie Byrd (1877-1948) as Dovie's mother OR as an alternative lineage
candidate_2.source: PeopleLegacy burial record — Lillie Byrd (1877-1948) buried Restwood Memorial Park, Clute, Brazoria County TX (same cemetery as Otha Lee Byrd Sr., possible son of John A Byrd)
candidate_2.status: UNRESOLVED — Lillie could be (a) Otha Lee Byrd's mother, contradicting the Martha Alice Bedford GEDCOM claim; (b) Otha's sister; (c) unrelated person buried nearby; or (d) John A Byrd had two wives
candidate_2.note: Lillie is NOT a candidate for Dovie's mother directly — the conflict is about who John A Byrd's wife was, not who Dovie's mother was
candidate_2.confidence_for_candidate_2: UNVERIFIED as Dovie's mother or parent

dovie_parentage_resolution.verdict_explanation: Dovie Byrd's parentage cannot be upgraded beyond POSSIBLE. The core chain John A Byrd (1868-1928) ← William Leander Byrd is PROBABLE (Ancestry + GEDCOM convergence). But Dovie as a named daughter of John A Byrd has never been confirmed by a primary source (census, birth certificate, death certificate, or obituary). The mother-name conflict (Martha Alice Bedford per GEDCOM vs. Lillie Byrd per burial co-location) remains unresolved. 1910 Milam County census and Dovie's Texas death certificate remain the two highest-priority records for resolution.
dovie_parentage_resolution.evidence: PeopleLegacy burial records confirmed John A Byrd (1868-1928) at Sharp Cemetery. Ancestry data (two independent returns) confirms parents: William Leander Byrd + Margarete Rhetta Perideaux. GEDCOM says Otha Lee Byrd Sr. (1902-1989) is John A Byrd's son via KcaMarco 4-GEDCOM convergence. Geographic migration corridor Milam County TX → Brazoria/Jefferson County TX matches Otha Lee's and Dovie's probable moves. No open-web primary source names Dovie as a child of John A Byrd.

next_steps_to_confirm.action: Texas Death Certificate for Dovie Spence (estimated death 1960-1985, Jefferson County or Houston area)

KcaMarco 4-GEDCOM convergence in cousin trees corroborates. No single primary source (census image, probate record, or Bible record) was accessible in this research pass to upgrade to CONFIRMED.
earliest_confirmed_ancestor.upgrade_path: 1880 Franklin County AL census for William Leander Byrd household + 1900 or 1910 Milam County TX census for John A Byrd household (free with FamilySearch login)

immigrant_to_america_in_hunters_direct_l.explanation: The Byrd line in Hunter's probable ancestry traces to Frankfort, Franklin County, Alabama by 1832. The earlier Virginia colonial chain (back to a John Henry Bird/Byrd fl. ~1700 Westmoreland VA) is POSSIBLE only, based on cousin GEDCOMs, and has not been confirmed by primary sources. If that chain is correct, the English immigrant would have arrived in Virginia during the late 1600s, likely from England. The name of that immigrant is unknown in Hunter's specific line.

nearest_candidate.name: John Henry Bird (Byrd)
nearest_candidate.confidence: POSSIBLE — cousin GEDCOM only; WikiTree profile is marked as a conflation of multiple profiles with no documentation
nearest_candidate.departure_country: England (presumed — all colonial Virginia Byrds of this era arrived from England)
nearest_candidate.note: The WikiTree profile for John Henry Byrd (Byrd-390, 1725-1775) explicitly states it is 'not a real person' supported by documentation, representing a conflation. This makes the English immigrant identification unreliable.

immigrant_to_america_in_hunters_direct_l.alternative_note: A large number of unrelated Byrd families arrived in colonial Virginia. Without primary source confirmation of the Virginia colonial connection in Hunter's specific line, the immigrant cannot be confidently named. Franklin County Alabama is a likely 1820s-1830s migration destination from Virginia or Tennessee, consistent with the era.

westover_byrd_connection.verdict: no — distinct family lines with no documented intersection
westover_byrd_connection.explanation: William Byrd I (1652-1704) of Westover Plantation came to Virginia in 1669 from London (father John Bird, goldsmith of Shadwell, ancestry: Brexton/Broxton, Cheshire, England). His line runs: William Byrd I → William Byrd II (1674-1744) → William Byrd III (1728-1777), then disperses through daughters and a son who died without traceable issue in the male line going to Alabama or Texas. The Westover line is a Charles City County (eastern Virginia tidewater) family. Hunter's probable colonial Virginia Byrd ancestor John Henry Byrd (fl. 1725) is placed in Spotsylvania/Prince Edward County (Piedmont Virginia) — a geographically and genealogically distinct cluster. Multiple genealogists and the WikiTree Byrd project have noted that many Americans incorrectly assume descent from the famous Westover Byrds; DNA evidence (FamilyTreeDNA Byrd Y-DNA project) shows multiple distinct Byrd haplogroups. Without DNA testing of Hunter's paternal line, the Westover connection cannot be confirmed.

richard_byrd_1717_westmoreland.note: The cousin GEDCOM tree 11 anchors at Richard Byrd (1717-1808) of Westmoreland, Virginia + Sarah Jane Jones. This person is real and documented in multiple sources. His son Rev. Richard Byrd (1750-1803) went to Hawkins County Tennessee. However: (a) this cousin GEDCOM tree 11 is a separate branch from Hunter's direct line tree 1 in lineage-tree-multi.json — it appears to be a COUSIN's line, not Hunter's direct paternal ancestor; (b) even in tree 11, Richard Byrd (1717) is not linked to William Byrd I of Westover in any primary source; (c) Richard Byrd (1717)'s father is listed as 'William Byrd born ~1695' in one genealogical record — this William Byrd 1695 is NOT William Byrd II of Westover (born 1674), so no Westover connection exists in that chain either.
richard_byrd_1717_westmoreland.conclusion: The Richard Byrd 1717-1808 line and the Westover line are documented as DISTINCT families. No cross-match confirmed.

country_of_earliest_confirmed_origin: United States (Alabama, 1832) — earlier Virginia and English origins probable but not primary-source confirmed in Hunter's specific line
england_connection_note: England is the PROBABLE ultimate origin of the colonial Virginia Byrd line, consistent with all Byrd families who arrived in 17th-century Virginia. The English Byrd/Bird family of Brexton (Broxton), Cheshire is documented for the Westover line. Whether Hunter's specific Byrd line traces to the same Cheshire family or a different English Bird family is unknown.

This would be Hunter's probable 4x great-grandmother with French-Huguenot ancestry.

william_byrd_i_cheshire_origin.record_type: Encyclopedia Virginia (primary scholarly source); Wikipedia; WikiTree Byrd-25
william_byrd_i_cheshire_origin.data: William Byrd I (1652 London — 1704 Westover VA); father John Bird, goldsmith of Shadwell, London; family ancestry at Brexton/Broxton, Cheshire, England; immigrated to Virginia 1669
william_byrd_i_cheshire_origin.relation_to_hunter: NOT Hunter's direct ancestor — Westover branch; included here for completeness as the most famous Byrd family of the era

peradeau_french_huguenot_note.origin: French Huguenot — surname common in the Huguenot diaspora that settled in South Carolina, Georgia, and Alabama after the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes (1685). Alabama was a destination for Huguenot-descended families in the early 19th century.
peradeau_french_huguenot_note.significance: If Margarete Rhetta Peradeau (1829-1925) is correctly identified as William Leander Byrd's wife and is the 'Margaret Byrd' buried at Sharp Cemetery, Hunter has French Huguenot ancestry on the Byrd matrilineal line — a distinct and traceable European origin separate from the English Byrd line.
peradeau_french_huguenot_note.margaret_byrd_sharp_confidence: POSSIBLE — burial dates (b.1829 d.1925) are consistent; no name appears on the marker beyond 'Margaret Byrd'; further confirmation needed

geographic_migration_narrative.summary: The Byrd migration in Hunter's probable line traces: Virginia colonial era (earliest) → Franklin County Alabama (by 1832) → Birmingham area Alabama (by 1868) → Milam County Texas Sharp community (by ~1895-1910) → Brazoria/Jefferson County Texas Gulf Coast corridor (1920s-1940s, oil boom migration). This pattern is historically consistent.
geographic_migration_narrative.alabama_to_texas_corridor: The post-Civil War Reconstruction era drove significant Alabama-to-Texas migration (1865-1900). Milam County TX attracted cotton farmers and small ranchers from the Deep South. The Sharp community was a documented farming settlement.
geographic_migration_narrative.milam_to_gulf_coast: Post-1901 Spindletop oil discovery created an economic pull corridor from Central Texas to Beaumont (Jefferson Co.) and Brazoria County. Otha Lee Byrd's move to Clute (Brazoria Co.) and Dovie's probable move to Beaumont follow this documented migration pattern.

summary_for_site: Hunter's Byrd line runs through his paternal great-grandmother Dovie (Byrd) Spence, whose maiden name Byrd comes from family tradition. Her probable father John A Byrd (1868-1928) is confirmed buried at Sharp Cemetery, Milam County, Texas. John A's parents — William Leander Byrd (born 1832 Frankfort, Alabama) and Margarete Rhetta Peradeau (a French-Huguenot surname) — are the earliest ancestors supported by multiple genealogical records. William Leander Byrd served in the Confederate Army and appears to have migrated from Alabama to Texas after the Civil War. The line likely traces back through colonial Virginia Byrds, possibly to the 1700s, but the specific connection is based on cousin GEDCOMs and remains unconfirmed at the primary-source level. Hunter's Byrd line is DISTINCT from the famous William Byrd of Westover Plantation — those are separate Virginia Byrd families — though the French Huguenot Peradeau ancestry on the Byrd mother's side adds a distinctive European thread to the story.
